edges.description: Loss of RPGR impairs ciliary delivery of outer-segment cargo, including opsin-rich membrane components needed to maintain photoreceptor architecture.
edges.source: RPGR dysfunction at the photoreceptor connecting cilium

edges.description: The common trafficking defect branches clinically into rod-predominant or cone-predominant disease depending on RPGR variant context, especially position within ORF15.
Both rod- and cone-predominant branches eventually converge on severe retinal degeneration with peripheral-predominant tissue loss and inner-retinal remodeling.
edges.target: Progressive photoreceptor loss and retinal remodeling
RPGR dysfunction at the photoreceptor connecting cilium

nodes.description: Impaired ciliary targeting prevents efficient movement of opsins and other cargo into the photoreceptor outer segment, producing structural disorganization and making both rod and cone photoreceptors vulnerable to degeneration.

evidence.snippet: We also observed disorganized photoreceptor outer-segment morphology and defective trafficking of opsins in the Rpgrko/Y::Cep290rd16/+ mice.

nodes.description: Late disease converges on severe rod and cone loss, peripheral-predominant retinal atrophy, and secondary inner-retinal remodeling with reactive gliosis.
nodes.id: Progressive photoreceptor loss and retinal remodeling

evidence.snippet: A histological examination of the donor eye showed disruption of retinal topology and stratification, with a more severe loss found in the peripheral regions. Reactive gliosis was seen in the inner layers of all regions.
Retinitis pigmentosa GTPase regulator (RPGR) gene sequence variants account for the vast majority of X linked retinitis pigmentosa (RP), which is one of the most severe forms of RP.

Variants in the X-linked retinitis pigmentosa GTPase regulator gene (RPGR) and, specifically, in its retinal opening reading frame-15 isoform (RPGRORF15) may cause rod-cone (RCD), cone, and cone-rod dystrophies (CDs and CRDs).
RPGR | HGNC:10295 | RPGR-related retinopathy | MONDO:0100437 | XL | Definitive

nodes.description: RPGR localizes to the photoreceptor connecting cilium, where it helps organize ciliary targeting and transport needed for outer-segment renewal in rods and cones. Loss or destabilization of RPGR creates the shared proximal defect across the RPGR family.
nodes.id: RPGR dysfunction at the photoreceptor connecting cilium

evidence.snippet: RPGR is involved in ciliary function, with ciliary dysfunction now recognised as the mechanism underlying a large proportion of inherited retinal disease.
Ablation of PDE6D blocked ciliary targeting of RPGR.

nodes.description: Despite a shared ciliary-trafficking defect, RPGR family disease diverges into rod-cone, cone-rod, and cone-dominant phenotypes. Variants toward the 3' half of RPGRORF15 are especially associated with cone-predominant presentations.

evidence.snippet: RPGR-ORF15 mutations were associated with COD/CORD and with a more severe phenotype in RP.
All were located in the 3' half of the RPGRORF15 transcript.
